segments.text: Hello everyone, I'm Pedro Nadal, a developer advocate at Autodesk,
working with Autodesk Platform Services.
But before joining Autodesk, I worked in architecture and engineering projects.
I was developing RevitAdins,
Automating Repetitive Tasks,
Supporting Beam Managers and Coordinators.
So what I'm sharing today is in theory.
It's coming straight from my hands-on experience as an architect.
Today, I want to hold you through what I see as the evolution of Beam Project setup.
We started with Manual Setup, you know, Enlist Checklist.
Then we build desktop automation, think of Dynamo Scripts and C-Sharp Addings.
Recently, we got Cloud Automation.
And we are starting to see our Phanomous orchestration.
Imagine the system thinking ahead for you.
I want to show you these evolutions through a problem I face myself many times.
I call it the Monday Morning Problem.
Okay, it's Monday Morning.
The Project Manager walks in and says,
Congratulations, we want three new projects, we start next week.
And Beam Managers know exactly what that means.
Before design even starts,
Project Setup, folder structure, discipline models, templates, sheets, links,
this is in Design Work.
It's operational overhead.
And it happens again and again and again.
We automated it right.
We have Repetadins, Dynamo Scripts, Excel Macros, batch processes.
But what still opens?
Rabbit.
Which means a machine runs it, a user triggers it, it depends on a UI,
it depends on a session.
It's automated, yes.
But it's not scalable.
What if instead of three new projects, the Project Manager says you want ten projects?
Now let me ask you another question.
What if you've Revit was a part of the setup workflow at all?
What if the control plane moved to the platform?
No UI, no desktop dependency, no someone clicking link model, just APIs?
That's where Autodesk Platform Services changes the game.
Revit remains the authoring tool, but provisioning becomes a cloud operation.
And once a provisioning leaves in the cloud, it becomes scalable.
Now I want to show you a real demo.
This is a small web application I built using .NET and APS.
In this example, the user selects target folder, a rapid template,
and can name the new models feeling a form with the name in the starters related to the folder.
And when they click create models,
it will run automation API behind the scenes and create a work item for each model.
Running in parallel.
Then after creating the models, we can see them in a links matrix.
And we can link them each other using a web browser even from a phone.
No Revit UI, no desktop session, everything runs in the cloud.
The Beam Manager doesn't open a file.
They provision infrastructure.
Once this setup becomes API-driven,
it becomes repeatable, scalable, version, even driven, and agent-ready.
Opening the door for orchestration and AI.
Let me show you another demo.
Once your automations are cloud-ready,
you can go one step forward and convert them into MCP tools and skills.
That AI agents can use to orchestrate autonomous operations.
Here you can see how an AI agent links two rapid models from simple instructions.
Now that you can talk to an agent to do things on your behalf,
you can go one another step forward and enter the agentic ecosystems.
Having the software engineering sector as a reference,
we see that you can create a space-like agents to perform tasks within their own responsibilities.
Then looking to our sector, we could do the same.
And create a space-like agent based on the roles of an AC project.
A project manager agent, a Beam Manager agent, a Beam Coordinator agent.
We also see how software development productivity has increased with these types of patterns.
Where a software engineer can review the work of an entire team of coding agents
without writing a single line of code.
What if we bring these patterns into AC?
A Beam Manager could review the work of a team of Beam Coordinator agents.
Once the entire agentic ecosystem is set up,
you can chain all these processes into agent workflows.
Imagine a new project is added to your ERP.
It triggers a PM agent and starts collecting the necessary information.
Then it talks to the Beam Manager agent and shares the project details.
The Beam Manager agents create the project informa and adds the users, manage permissions, etc.
Then the Beam Coordinator agent to their part, create the models, links disciplines, publish models,
and you can add as many agents as necessary to the workflow.
Autodesk Platform Services enables you to remove desktop and UI dependencies.
Provision project life infrastructure
and open the door for orchestration and AI.
Thank you.
